title: The Macro Minute: How will the “President Trump vs. Powell Fed” saga play out?

support for fiscal dominance, and b

loose with increasing frequency, I might

just generated a bearish signal for the

slowly over the next month. Everyone
who's wellversed in history knows that
every society in recorded history has
reached these fourth turning style
tipping points. The beauty of our
American democracy is that we always get
really close to the edge and find a
solution before falling off the
proverbial cliff. Kiss and Dr. Mo are
two of our core contributions to the
developing solution set. Are Kiss and

not. Of course not. But perfection is
neither attainable nor reasonable at our
generous price points. We didn't start

expressed the view that the Fed should
lower the short-term Fed funds rate due
to slowing growth in the private sector
labor income and what appears to be a
softening labor market. Additionally,
from a structural standpoint, given the
geopolitical driven supply demand
imbalance in the Treasury bond market,
the term premium demanded by investors
is rising and the fair value of the
10-year Treasury note is about 5.5%.
Even if the Fed lowers the Fed funds
rate, there is no guarantee the rest of
the curve will also decline. And in
fact, the 10-year note and 30-year bond
will likely rise uh and may have a
tightening effect on credit creation and
financial conditions. In addition, won't
the equity market likely have a
correction if the 10-year Treasury note
reaches 5%? Your thoughts on these
issues would be appreciated. So, uh
big-time question.
This is excellent question. Um you know,
these are the types of questions that
obviously fielding from institutional
investors throughout the week. And so,
uh, just want to commend the the new
client for, you know, the framing of
which, uh, framing this question so
thoughtfully and elegantly because,
again, they're touching on a lot of
different things that I think, uh, we
need to unpack here. Uh, so I think the
the starting, uh, I think we need to to
sort of clear the deck chairs for a bit
in terms of how the bond market usually
reacts to interest rate cuts. um if we
saw uh you know some form of politicized
rate cutting by the Fed or if the Fed
just started cutting rates um you know I
think those are two different outcomes.
One if the Fed just started cutting
rates because it you know enough FOMC
market participants viewed the one-off
price level change uh associated with
tariffs is a less of a risk to inflation
as the already observable slowdown in
private sector labor income is to the
the the the durability of the business
cycle and their maximum employment
mandate. they could all come around to
that especially if they start to see
more and more uh deteriorating labor
data in the context of our U-shaped
economy. You know, if the Fed is just
cutting rates because, you know, the
labor market is telling them to cut
rates and they can look through what is
very clearly not inflationary um price
adjustments from tariffs. You know, this
is no academic research would tell you
it's inflationary. They're just
anchoring on the the inflation
expectations genie that they let out of
the bottle with bad policy
anyway. So, I don't want to drag too
much, but if they just cut rates
because, you know, they need to cut
rates, then the bond market in terms of
the interest rates uh both on the 10
year and 38 will probably actually go
down. Probably not as much as the rate
the rate cuts themselves. So, you'd
probably see some bull steepening of the
yield curve in that context. If we saw
politicized rate cuts, which is probably
where we're just highly likely where
we're headed at some point over the next
few years, then you could easily see an
adverse reaction uh in the bond uh in
the in the in the longing of the uh
Treasury curve in terms of uh something
similar to to what we saw play out last
fall. But I will caveat that by saying
uh and I'll take you to my uh Bloomberg
launchpad here and just show you uh last
fall is a bit different than where we
were today. If you recall last fall, we
had a over more than a 100 basis point
decline in the 10-year Treasury yield
prior to the Fed cutting interest rates.
Uh the 10-year Treasury yield peaked uh
in uh late April at at a high of 4.74%.
It declined all the way to a low in in
in midepptember to a low of 3.6%. So we
saw 114 basis point decline in the
10-year Treasury yield prior to the Fed
cutting interest rates. Uh and so uh we
saw the Fed cut interest rates. In fact,
I was on our friend Charles Payne's show
this week here telling people to sell
bonds because the Fed was about to cut,
you know, the rate. Uh they had this,
you know, very narrow window to sort of
sneak in a bunch of rate cuts if they
wanted to. Uh and it was probably going
to extend the duration of the business
cycle and that's exactly what the bond
market reacted to. The Fed got the memo,
started cutting interest rates and
started to uh you know, ease policy in a
way that the bond market took as a
signal that the bond that the the
business cycle would get extended. uh
which is exactly what we were calling
for in our research in August and
September. Uh and so we saw another back
up in the interest rate. I want to say
we so we rallied from 3.6 on the 10-year
to a high of 4 uh 81 uh in uh in early
January. And so in our opinion, we think
that, you know, the bond market reacted
appropriately. You know, we had a growth
scare. The Fed reacted to the growth
scare and then the bond market sold off
as a function of the economy being much
better than it than investors thought it
was down here in August and September uh
when we were again having to defend our
resilient US economy theme again. How
many times have we had to defend our
resilience economy theme since we
authored it in the summer of 2022. I
mean that was like the we had defend it
then we had to defend it then we had to
defend it then we're defending it now.
Um, and so, uh, I I just think investors
need to be aware that, uh, just because
of what happened last fall, you just you
shouldn't just automatically assume that
if the Fed lowers the policy rate, 10
year and 30-year, uh, interest rates are
going to go higher. That's not normal.
That's not a normal dynamic. You the,
you know, there's a thing called role in
the bond market. And so, you know, fixed
income investors are, you know, they
like making money. They don't like, you
know, they don't like telling
narratives. They like making money. So
uh ultimately you could easily see the
bond market rally uh if the Fed is
cutting rates for the right reasons. Now
if they're cutting for the wrong reasons
then you could see turn premium wide and
you could see inflation expectations go
up uh which is likely likely to happen
and ultimately you're going to require
some form of durable Fed yield curve
control to fix that which is which is
where we think we're headed. We think
that's ultimately the the end point uh
of this whole process. But it's not,
like I said, it's this is going to be a
multi-year process. Could be 5 to 10
years in the making in terms of the
duration of this for turning where we're
actually using the Fed's balance sheet
to help capitalize the US government
because we're running out of domestic
and foreign creditors. Um, and so uh
